sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Peritoneal mesothelioma (PeM) is a rare cancer, with an estimated 300-400 new cases annually in the US, representing approximately 15% of all mesotheliomas. The mean age at diagnosis is about 69 years. The 1-year overall survival (OS) is approximately 46%, and the 5-year OS is about 20%, with cure being rare [11, 121-124]. Treatment philosophy centers on a multimodal approach, primarily involving cytoreductive surgery (CRS) with intraperitoneal chemotherapy for eligible patients, or systemic therapy for those who are not surgical candidates. Histologic subtype (epithelioid, biphasic, sarcomatoid) is a critical determinant of prognosis and treatment strategy. Epithelioid histology carries a better prognosis (median OS ~55 months with CRS) compared to biphasic (median OS ~13 months) [15]. All treatment decisions should be made by a multidisciplinary team with experience in peritoneal mesothelioma [12, 21].

edges.label: Evaluate PS, histology, disease extent, resectability
Medically operable, epithelioid, unicavitary, complete CRS possible
Inoperable, high-risk histology (biphasic/sarcomatoid), bicavitary, incomplete CRS, recurrent, PS 0-2 or PS 3-4
Low risk: no nodal met, Ki-67 ≤9%, etc.
High risk: nodal met, Ki-67 >9%, etc.

cards.title: Curative/Prognostic
cards.detail: Primary treatment with cytoreductive surgery (CRS) + hyperthermic intraperitoneal chemotherapy (HIPEC). For low-risk features, surveillance follows. For high-risk features, adjuvant systemic therapy is considered [PEM-2, PEM-D].

cards.title: Palliative/Non-curative
cards.detail: Systemic therapy as primary treatment. Surgery may be considered in select cases (e.g., low-volume biphasic disease). Best supportive care is recommended for patients with performance status (PS) 3–4 [PEM-2, PEM-B].
